summary: Dan Shapiro demystifies seemingly irrational VC behavior by tracing it back to fund economics. He explains that VCs invest other people's money from 10-year funds, earning 2% annual management fees plus 20% of profits, which creates incentive structures that drive counterintuitive decisions. The math of needing 3.6x returns just to match the S&P 500 explains why VCs chase 10x outcomes, push founders to take more capital than needed, block reasonable exits, and herd into the same investment sectors. Shapiro argues that once you understand how VCs get paid, their behavior becomes entirely predictable rather than insane.
key_points: VCs take their biggest risk when closing a fund; everything after is risk mitigation with other people's money
The 2-20 fee structure (2% annual management fee, 20% carry) incentivizes raising larger funds even though smaller funds empirically outperform
A 10x return target isn't greed — after accounting for failures, management fees, and compounding, it's what's needed just to match the S&P 500
VCs block seemingly great exits because returns are calculated against reserved capital, not just invested capital
Herd investing reduces thesis risk and provides political cover with LPs when investments fail
money_quotes: It's crazy but true: if you know how a VC gets paid, you can pretty much read their mind.
Modern venture capital is one shining moment of derring-do taking followed by 10 years of risk mitigation. It's sort of like breaking a mirror.
Folks who put their own dimes on the line are known as angel investors, not VCs. Venture Capital is strictly a game of other people's property.

When you hear that VCs aim for a 10x return, it's not greed – it's because if a third of their companies fail and a third just barely get them their money back, a 10x return on the winners puts them in the same place as the S&P 500!
If you took the leap to invest in caffeinated soap all by yourself, good luck explaining that to your LPs.
There's simply no way a person can surprise you if you know how to read their mind.
Golf games may be in danger of abbreviation.
key_insight: VC behavior that appears irrational to entrepreneurs is entirely predictable once you understand fund economics: the 2-20 fee structure, 10-year fund timelines, and the math required to beat the S&P 500.
